subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the reaction :
$$
\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{S}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
The rate equation for this reaction is rate $=\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}\right]$ Which of these mechanisms is/are consistent with this rate equation?
(A) $\mathrm{Cl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{Cl}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \quad$ (slow)
$$
\mathrm{Cl}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{S} \text { (fast) }
$$
(B) $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \quad$ (fast equilibrium) $\mathrm{Cl}_2+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{S} \quad$ (slow)
question:
Consider the reaction :
$$
\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{S}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
The rate equation for this reaction is rate $=\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}\right]$ Which of these mechanisms is/are consistent with this rate equation?
(A) $\mathrm{Cl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{Cl}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \quad$ (slow)
$$
\mathrm{Cl}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{S} \text { (fast) }
$$
(B) $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \quad$ (fast equilibrium) $\mathrm{Cl}_2+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{S} \quad$ (slow)
solution:
Rate equation is to be derived wrt slow
Step $\therefore$ from mechanism (A)
$$
\text { Rate }=\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}\right]
$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
First order gas phase reaction $A \rightarrow B + C$
$p_i =$ initial pressure of gas A, $p_t =$ total pressure of the reaction mixture at time $t$
Expression of rate constant ($k$) is
question:
First order gas phase reaction $A \rightarrow B + C$
$p_i =$ initial pressure of gas A, $p_t =$ total pressure of the reaction mixture at time $t$
Expression of rate constant ($k$) is
solution:
The given reaction is $A(g) \rightarrow B(g) + C(g)$

At $t = 0$, the initial pressure of A is $p_i$ and the pressures of B and C are $0$.

At time $t$, let the decrease in pressure of A be $x$.
Pressure of A $= p_i - x$
Pressure of B $= x$
Pressure of C $= x$

Total pressure at time $t$ is $p_t = (p_i - x) + x + x = p_i + x$

From this, we get $x = p_t - p_i$

The partial pressure of A at time $t$ is $P_A = p_i - x = p_i - (p_t - p_i) = 2p_i - p_t$

For a first order reaction, the rate constant $k$ is given by

$k = \dfrac{1}{t}\ln\dfrac{P_{A,0}}{P_A}$

Substituting the values, we get

$k = \dfrac{1}{t}\ln\dfrac{p_i}{2p_i - p_t}$

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{t}\ln\dfrac{p_i}{2p_i - p_t}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
If a reaction follows the Arrhenius equation, the plot $\ln k$

vs $\frac{1}{(\mathrm{RT})}$ gives straight line with a gradient $(-\mathrm{y})$ unit.

The energy required to activate the reactant is:
question:
If a reaction follows the Arrhenius equation, the plot $\ln k$

vs $\frac{1}{(\mathrm{RT})}$ gives straight line with a gradient $(-\mathrm{y})$ unit.

The energy required to activate the reactant is:
solution:
From Arrhenius equation,

$k=A e^{-E_{n} / R T}$

$\ln k=\ln A-\frac{E_{a}}{R T}$

slope $=-y$ (given) $-y=-E_{s}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=y$

The rate expression for the reverse reaction is:
question: For the reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>→</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced></math> the observed rate expression is, rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub><msup><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mi>NO</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced></math> . The rate expression for the reverse reaction is:
options: <math><mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced><msup><mrow><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mtext>NO</mtext></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math>

<math><mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced><msup><mrow><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mtext>H</mtext></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math>
solution:
K eq = k f k b = N 2 H 2 O 2 H 2 2 [NO] 2

At equilibrium rf=rb
 
k f H 2 [NO] 2 = k b N 2 H 2 O 2 H 2

Hence, rate expression for reverse reaction.

= k b N 2 H 2 O 2 H 2

subject: Chemistry
q: If 50 % of a reaction occurs in 100 second and 75 % of the reaction occurs in 200 second, the order of this reaction is:
question: If 50 % of a reaction occurs in 100 second and 75 % of the reaction occurs in 200 second, the order of this reaction is:
solution:
50% of a reaction occurs in 100 second and 75% of the reaction occurs in 200 second, the order of this reaction is 1.

Let the initial reactant concentration is 1 M.

In the first 100 seconds, reactant concentration is reduced to 50% or 1×50100​=0.5 M.

In the second 100 seconds (total 200 seconds from start), reactant concentration is reduced to 100−75=25% or  1× 25100​ =0.25 M..

Thus, we observe that after each 100 second period, the reactant concentration is reduced to one half. Hence, 100 seconds is the half-life period and is independent of reactant concentration. This is characteristic of the first-order reaction.



First-order reaction as the half-life is constant.

subject: Chemistry
q: Time required for completion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of first order reaction is ________ times of half life <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></math> of the reaction
question: Time required for completion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of first order reaction is ________ times of half life <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></math> of the reaction
solution:
For first order reaction:
kt=logA0At

A0=Initial concentration of A.

At=Concentration of A at time t.

t99.9%t50%=x=log100100-99.9log10050     =log1000.1log 2     = log 1000log 2    =30.3=10

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following single step reaction in gas phase at constant temperature.
\(2 \mathrm{~A}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}\)
The initial rate of the reaction is recorded as \(\mathrm{r}_1\) when the reaction starts with \(1.5 \mathrm{~atm}\) pressure of \(\mathrm{A}\) and \(0.7 \mathrm{~atm}\) pressure of \(\mathrm{B}\). After some time, the rate \(\mathrm{r}_2\) is recorded when the pressure of \(\mathrm{C}\) becomes \(0.5 \mathrm{~atm}\). The ratio \(\mathrm{r}_1: \mathrm{r}_2\) is ______ \(\times 10^{-1}\). (Nearest integer)
question:
Consider the following single step reaction in gas phase at constant temperature.
\(2 \mathrm{~A}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}\)
The initial rate of the reaction is recorded as \(\mathrm{r}_1\) when the reaction starts with \(1.5 \mathrm{~atm}\) pressure of \(\mathrm{A}\) and \(0.7 \mathrm{~atm}\) pressure of \(\mathrm{B}\). After some time, the rate \(\mathrm{r}_2\) is recorded when the pressure of \(\mathrm{C}\) becomes \(0.5 \mathrm{~atm}\). The ratio \(\mathrm{r}_1: \mathrm{r}_2\) is ______ \(\times 10^{-1}\). (Nearest integer)
solution:
$2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$
$\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{r}_1 & 1.5 \mathrm{~atm} \quad 0.7 \mathrm{~atm} \\ \mathrm{r}_2 & 0.5 \mathrm{~atm} \quad 0.2 \mathrm{~atm} \quad 0.5 \mathrm{~atm} \\ & \because \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{K}\left[\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}\right]^2\left[\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}\right] \\ & \mathrm{r}_1=\mathrm{K}[1.5]^2[0.7] \\ & \mathrm{r}_2=\mathrm{K}[0.5]^2[0.2] \\ & \frac{\mathrm{r}_1}{\mathrm{r}_2}=9 \times \frac{7}{2}=31.5=315 \times 10^{-1} \end{array}$ Ans. 315

subject: Chemistry
q:
For reaction $A \rightarrow P$, rate constant $k = 1.5 \times 10^3$ s$^{-1}$ at $27°$C. If activation energy for the above reaction is $60$ kJ mol$^{-1}$, then the temperature (in $°$C) at which rate constant, $k = 4.5 \times 10^3$ s$^{-1}$ is _______. (Nearest integer)
Given : $\log 2 = 0.30$, $\log 3 = 0.48$, $R = 8.3$ J K$^{-1}$ mol$^{-1}$, $\ln 10 = 2.3$
question:
For reaction $A \rightarrow P$, rate constant $k = 1.5 \times 10^3$ s$^{-1}$ at $27°$C. If activation energy for the above reaction is $60$ kJ mol$^{-1}$, then the temperature (in $°$C) at which rate constant, $k = 4.5 \times 10^3$ s$^{-1}$ is _______. (Nearest integer)
Given : $\log 2 = 0.30$, $\log 3 = 0.48$, $R = 8.3$ J K$^{-1}$ mol$^{-1}$, $\ln 10 = 2.3$
solution:
Using the Arrhenius equation:

$\log \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = \dfrac{E_a}{2.3 R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$

Given:
$k_1 = 1.5 \times 10^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$
$k_2 = 4.5 \times 10^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$
$T_1 = 27^\circ\text{C} = 300 \text{ K}$
$E_a = 60 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} = 60000 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$
$R = 8.3 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$

Substituting the values into the equation:

$\log \left( \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^3}{1.5 \times 10^3} \right) = \dfrac{60000}{2.3 \times 8.3} \left( \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$

$\log 3 = \dfrac{60000}{19.09} \left( \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$

$0.48 = \dfrac{60000}{19.09} \left( \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{0.48 \times 19.09}{60000}$

$\dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{9.1632}{60000} = 1.5272 \times 10^{-4}$

$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{300} - 1.5272 \times 10^{-4}$

$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = 3.3333 \times 10^{-3} - 0.1527 \times 10^{-3} = 3.1806 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$

$T_2 = \dfrac{1}{3.1806 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 314.4 \text{ K}$

Converting to Celsius:
$T_2 = 314.4 - 273 = 41.4^\circ\text{C}$

The nearest integer is $41$.

Answer: $41$

subject: Chemistry
q:
A person's wound was exposed to some bacteria and then bacteria growth started to happen at the same place. The wound was later treated with some antibacterial medicine and the rate of bacterial decay (r) was found to be proportional with the square of the existing number of bacteria at any instance. Which of the following set of graphs correctly represents the 'before' and 'after' situation of the application of the medicine?
[Given : $\mathrm{N}=$ No. of bacteria, $\mathrm{t}=$ time, bacterial growth follows $I^{\text {st }}$ order kinetics.]
question:
A person's wound was exposed to some bacteria and then bacteria growth started to happen at the same place. The wound was later treated with some antibacterial medicine and the rate of bacterial decay (r) was found to be proportional with the square of the existing number of bacteria at any instance. Which of the following set of graphs correctly represents the 'before' and 'after' situation of the application of the medicine?
[Given : $\mathrm{N}=$ No. of bacteria, $\mathrm{t}=$ time, bacterial growth follows $I^{\text {st }}$ order kinetics.]
solution:
*Before applying medicine
$\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]$ (First order growth) (Rate law)
$\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{~A}_0}=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{~N}_0}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{Kt}}$
*After applying medicine

$\mathrm{r}=-\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]^2 \quad$ (Rate law)
$y=\mathrm{Kx}^2$ Parabola

subject: Chemistry
q:
For the thermal decomposition of reactant $\mathrm{AB}(\mathrm{g})$, the following plot is constructed.

The half life of the reaction is ' $x^{\prime} \min$. $x=$ $\_\_\_\_$ min. (Nearest integer)
question:
For the thermal decomposition of reactant $\mathrm{AB}(\mathrm{g})$, the following plot is constructed.

The half life of the reaction is ' $x^{\prime} \min$. $x=$ $\_\_\_\_$ min. (Nearest integer)
solution:
From the concentration vs. time graph, the thermal decomposition of AB(g) shows a linear decrease in concentration, indicating zeroth-order kinetics.
Initial concentration: [AB]₀ = 0.6 M at t = 0 s.
Reading from the graph: [AB] = 0.5 M at t = 200 s.
Rate of decomposition: Δ[AB]/Δt = (0.6 - 0.5) M / 200 s = 0.0005 M/s.
For zeroth-order kinetics: [AB] = [AB]₀ - kt.
Half-life occurs when [AB] = [AB]₀/2 = 0.3 M.
0.3 = 0.6 - 0.0005 × t₁/₂.
t₁/₂ = 0.3 / 0.0005 = 600 s = 10 min.

subject: Chemistry
q: Higher order (>3) reactions are rare due to:
question: Higher order (>3) reactions are rare due to:
options: Loss of active species on collision.
Low probability of simultaneous collision of all the reacting species.
Increase in entropy and activation energy as more molecules are involved.
Shifting of equilibrium towards reactants due to elastic collisions.
solution: Probability of an event involving more than three molecules in a collision are remote.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The rate constant for a first order reaction is given by the following equation :

lnk=33.24-2.0×104 K T

The Activation energy for the reaction is given by kJmol-1. (In Nearest integer) (Given : R=8.3 J K-1 mol-1)
question:
The rate constant for a first order reaction is given by the following equation :

lnk=33.24-2.0×104 K T

The Activation energy for the reaction is given by kJmol-1. (In Nearest integer) (Given : R=8.3 J K-1 mol-1)
solution:
lnk=lnA-EART

Given: lnk=33.24-2.0×104 T

∴ on comparing EAR=2.0×104

∴ EA=2.0×104×R

⇒EA=2.0×104×8.3 J

⇒EA=16.6×104 J=166kJ

subject: Chemistry
q:
2NO+2H2→N2+2H2O

The above reaction has been studied at 800 °C. The related data are given in the table below

Reaction serial number Initial pressure ofH2/kPa  Initial Pressure
of NO/kPa		 Initial rate -dpdt/kPa/s
1 65.6 40.0 0.135
2 65.6 20.1 0.033
3 38.6 65.6 0.214
4 19.2 65.6 0.106

The order of the reaction with respect to NO is____
question:
2NO+2H2→N2+2H2O

The above reaction has been studied at 800 °C. The related data are given in the table below

Reaction serial number Initial pressure ofH2/kPa  Initial Pressure
of NO/kPa		 Initial rate -dpdt/kPa/s
1 65.6 40.0 0.135
2 65.6 20.1 0.033
3 38.6 65.6 0.214
4 19.2 65.6 0.106

The order of the reaction with respect to NO is____
solution:
The rate law for the reaction 2NO+2H2→N2+2H2O is

r=kH2mNOn

The powers of the concentration terms m, n are the order with respect to hydrogen and nitric oxide respectively.

r1=65.6m40.0n=0.135r2=65.6m20.1n=0.033r3=38.6m65.6n=0.214r4=19.2m65.6n=0.106

r1r2=65.6m40.0n65.6m20.1n=0.1350.033=4⇒2n=4⇒n=2

Hence, the order with respect to NO is 2.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements : Statement I: The rate law for the reaction \(A+B \rightarrow C\) is rate \((r)=k[A]^2[B]\). When the concentration of both A and B is doubled, the reaction rate is increased " \(x\) " times. Statement II :
The figure is showing "the variation in concentration against time plot" for a " \(y\) " order reaction. The Value of \(x+y\) is ______
question:
Given below are two statements : Statement I: The rate law for the reaction \(A+B \rightarrow C\) is rate \((r)=k[A]^2[B]\). When the concentration of both A and B is doubled, the reaction rate is increased " \(x\) " times. Statement II :
The figure is showing "the variation in concentration against time plot" for a " \(y\) " order reaction. The Value of \(x+y\) is ______
solution:
$\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]^2|\mathrm{~B}|$ if conc. are doubled $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{r}^{\prime}=\mathrm{K}[2 \mathrm{~A}]^2[2 \mathrm{~B}]^1 \\ & \mathrm{r}^{\prime}=8 \mathrm{r} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=8 \end{aligned}$

p (mm Hg) 50 100 200 400

The order of the reaction is
question:
A student has studied the decomposition of a gas AB3 at 25°C. He obtained the following data

p (mm Hg) 50 100 200 400
Relative t12s 4 2 1 0.5

The order of the reaction is
solution:
By observing the data given, by doubling the pressure, half-life is reduced to half. This result is following second order kinetics. Mathematically it can be proved as follows,

The half-life and initial pressure of nth order reaction can be related as follows,

t12∝Po1-n

t121t122=P011-nP021-n

⇒42=501001-n

⇒2=121-n

⇒2=2n-1

⇒n-1=1

⇒n=2

Hence, the order of the reaction is second order.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following gas phase reaction being carried out in a closed vessel at $25°C$.
$2A(g) \rightarrow 4B(g) + C(g)$
time (min)total pressure of the system (mm Hg)
$30$$300$
$\infty$$600$
The pressure of $C(g)$ at $30$ minutes time interval would be _______ mm Hg. (nearest integer)
question:
Consider the following gas phase reaction being carried out in a closed vessel at $25°C$.
$2A(g) \rightarrow 4B(g) + C(g)$
time (min)total pressure of the system (mm Hg)
$30$$300$
$\infty$$600$
The pressure of $C(g)$ at $30$ minutes time interval would be _______ mm Hg. (nearest integer)
solution:
For the given gas phase reaction:

$2A(g) \rightarrow 4B(g) + C(g)$

Let the initial pressure of $A(g)$ be $P_0$.

At $t = 0$: $P_A = P_0$, $P_B = 0$, $P_C = 0$

At $t = t$: $P_A = P_0 - 2p$, $P_B = 4p$, $P_C = p$

Total pressure at time $t$, $P_t = P_0 - 2p + 4p + p = P_0 + 3p$

At $t = \infty$, the reaction is complete, so $P_A = 0 \Rightarrow 2p = P_0 \Rightarrow p = \dfrac{P_0}{2}$

Total pressure at $t = \infty$, $P_{\infty} = 4\left(\dfrac{P_0}{2}\right) + \dfrac{P_0}{2} = \dfrac{5P_0}{2}$

Given $P_{\infty} = 600$ mm Hg:

$\dfrac{5P_0}{2} = 600 \Rightarrow P_0 = 240$ mm Hg

At $t = 30$ min, $P_t = 300$ mm Hg:

$P_0 + 3p = 300$

$240 + 3p = 300 \Rightarrow 3p = 60 \Rightarrow p = 20$ mm Hg

The pressure of $C(g)$ at $30$ minutes is $p = 20$ mm Hg.

Answer: $20$

subject: Chemistry
q: $t_{100\%}$ is the time required for the $100\%$ completion of the reaction while $t_{1/2}$ is the time required for $50\%$ of the reaction to be completed. Which of the following option correctly represents the relation between $t_{100\%}$ and $t_{1/2}$ for zero and first order reactions respectively?
question: $t_{100\%}$ is the time required for the $100\%$ completion of the reaction while $t_{1/2}$ is the time required for $50\%$ of the reaction to be completed. Which of the following option correctly represents the relation between $t_{100\%}$ and $t_{1/2}$ for zero and first order reactions respectively?
solution:
For a zero-order reaction:
The integrated rate law is given by $t = \dfrac{[A]_0 - [A]_t}{k}$.

For $50\%$ completion ($t_{1/2}$), $[A]_t = \dfrac{[A]_0}{2}$:
$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{[A]_0 - \dfrac{[A]_0}{2}}{k} = \dfrac{[A]_0}{2k}$

For $100\%$ completion ($t_{100\%}$), $[A]_t = 0$:
$t_{100\%} = \dfrac{[A]_0 - 0}{k} = \dfrac{[A]_0}{k}$

Comparing the two, we get:
$t_{100\%} = 2t_{1/2}$

For a first-order reaction:
The integrated rate law is given by $t = \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\left(\dfrac{[A]_0}{[A]_t}\right)$.

For $100\%$ completion ($t_{100\%}$), $[A]_t = 0$:
$t_{100\%} = \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\left(\dfrac{[A]_0}{0}\right) = \infty$

A first-order reaction takes infinite time for $100\%$ completion. Among the given choices, this is symbolically represented as $\infty$ or $(t_{1/2})^\infty$.

Thus, the correct relation is $t_{100\%} = 2t_{1/2}$ for zero-order and $t_{100\%} = \infty$ for first-order.

Answer: $t_{100\%} = 2t_{1/2}$ and $t_{100\%} = (t_{1/2})^\infty$

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following statement is not true for radioactive decay?
question: Which of the following statement is not true for radioactive decay?
options: Decay constant increases with increase in temperature.
Amount of radioactive substance remained after three half lives is $\frac{1}{8}$ th of original amount.
Decay constant does not depend upon temperature.
Half life is $\ln 2$ times of $\frac{1}{\text { rate constant }}$.
solution: For radioactive decay, decay constant does not depend upon temperature because for radioactive decay activation energy is zero.

If the ratio of the activation energies of the forward <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub></mfenced></math> and reverse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub></mfenced></math> reactions is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> then:
question: For the equilibrium, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>⇌</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo> </mo><mi>is</mi><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math> . If the ratio of the activation energies of the forward <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub></mfenced></math> and reverse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub></mfenced></math> reactions is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> then:
solution:
Activation energy is the minimum amount of energy that must be provided for compounds to result in a chemical reaction.

Ag⇌Bg ; ∆H=-40 kJ/mole

∆H=Eaf-Eab=-40 kJ

EafEab=23

⇒ Eaf=23Eab

⇒23 Eab-Eab=-Eab3=-40 kJ

Eab=+120 kJ

Eaf=23×120=80 kJ

comparing units

question: For a reaction scheme <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mover accent="false"><mo>→</mo><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></mrow></mover><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mover accent="false"><mo>→</mo><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></mrow></mover><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> if the net rate of formation of B is set to be zero then the concentration of B is given by:
solution:
dBdt= Rate of formation of B- Rate of dissociation of B

dBdt=k1A-k2B

Given;   dBdt=0

 k1A-k2B=0

B=k1Ak2
